title: Why Leaves Change Color
content:
Every spring and summer, the trees are full of bright green leaves. They give you shade on hot days and rustle pleasantly in the breeze. But as summer ends and the days grow shorter, something remarkable begins to happen to those green leaves.

Leaves are green because of a special ingredient inside them called chlorophyll. Chlorophyll is like a tiny factory. It uses sunlight, water from the soil, and air to make food for the tree. All spring and summer long, chlorophyll works hard, feeding the whole tree.

When autumn arrives, the days get shorter and the nights get longer. The tree senses these changes and knows that cold winter is coming. The tree begins to slow down and seal off its leaves, stopping the flow of water and food to them.

Without water and nutrients, the chlorophyll breaks down and fades away. As the green disappears, something surprising is revealed: yellow and orange colors were hiding in the leaf the whole time! They were always there, but the bright green chlorophyll was covering them up.

Red is a little different. As the tree seals off its leaves, sugars get trapped inside them. Sunlight turns those trapped sugars into red and purple pigments. The more sugar and sunshine, the brighter and redder the color.

After putting on their brilliant show of color, the leaves fall to the ground. The bare tree stands all winter, resting quietly and saving its energy. But come spring, the warmth and longer days will wake the tree again, and fresh green leaves will unfurl to start the whole wonderful cycle once more.

author: Educational
